# within-mcp-auth

One function, one handler mount. The SDK owns auth, quota, usage reporting, subscriber detection, conversion tracking, session management, transport, and cross-vendor network discovery. Your code writes tools and runs a server — the SDK does the rest.

---

## Install

```bash
npm install git+https://github.com/tiwanaca/within-sdk.git#<commit-sha>
```

Pin a **full commit SHA**, not a branch or tag — Render's `node_modules` cache will otherwise serve a stale build.

Alongside the SDK, your project must install:

- `zod@^4` — you pass your own instance via the `zod` config field.
- `@modelcontextprotocol/server` — your factory returns one of its `McpServer` instances.

The SDK itself only depends on `jose`.

---

## Integration — 3 steps

### Step 1. Serve your MCP config

At `GET /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`, your server must respond with a [RFC 9728 Protected Resource Metadata](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9728) document that points at With.in as the authorization server:

```json
{
  "resource": "https://your-domain.com/mcp",
  "authorization_servers": ["https://gateway.getwith.in"],
  "scopes_supported": ["tools:read", "tools:write"],
  "bearer_methods_supported": ["header"]
}
```

You don't hand-write this. **`within.prmHandler` generates it at request time** from your `createWithinHandler` config — deriving `resource` from the incoming request's host + `X-Forwarded-Proto` so the RFC 9728 §3.3 byte-equality check passes under any deployment hostname.

MCP clients discover With.in automatically from this document. This is the entire "URL swap" — adding With.in's `authorization_servers` entry is what turns on network access for your vendor.

### Step 2. Build your MCP server

This is stock MCP. Two structural asks:

1. **Wrap it in a factory.** The SDK calls it once per new session — each session gets a fresh server.
2. **Don't register `find_vendor_for_task` or `within__use` yourself.** The SDK injects those at runtime.

```typescript
// server.ts
import { McpServer } from '@modelcontextprotocol/server';
import { z } from 'zod';

export function createServer(): McpServer {
  const server = new McpServer({ name: 'acme-invoicing', version: '1.0.0' });

  server.registerTool('create_invoice', {
    description: 'Create a draft invoice.',
    inputSchema: z.object({ customer: z.string(), amount: z.number() }),
  }, async ({ customer, amount }) => ({
    content: [{ type: 'text', text: `invoice created for ${customer}` }],
  }));

  return server;
}
```

### Step 3. Mount the handler

```typescript
import { createServer as createHttpServer } from 'node:http';
import { z } from 'zod';
import { createWithinHandler } from 'within-mcp-auth';
import { createServer } from './server.js';
import { findUserByEmail } from './db.js';

const AUTH_SERVER = 'https://gateway.getwith.in';

const within = createWithinHandler({
  jwksUrl: `${AUTH_SERVER}/.well-known/jwks.json`,
  authServerUrl: AUTH_SERVER,
  vendorSlug: 'acme',
  resourceUrl: 'https://acme-invoicing.com/mcp',
  vendorHostUrl: 'https://acme-invoicing.com',
  zod: z,
  createMcpServer: () => createServer(),

  // Recognize your paying customers when they arrive via With.in tokens.
  // Without this, paying users hit trial walls — see "The isSubscriber hook" below.
  isSubscriber: async (email) => !!(await findUserByEmail(email)),
});

createHttpServer(async (req, res) => {
  const url = new URL(req.url ?? '/', 'http://x');

  if (url.pathname === '/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource') return within.prmHandler(req, res);
  if (url.pathname === '/subscribe')                             return within.subscribeHandler(req, res);
  if (url.pathname === '/mcp')                                   return within.mcpHandler(req, res);

  res.writeHead(404).end('Not found');
}).listen(4100);
```

That's the whole integration.

---

## The `isSubscriber` hook — read this

> **Wire `isSubscriber` on day one.** Without it, your paying customers who arrive through With.in (e.g. from a cross-vendor `within__use` call) get treated as trial users, burn through a trial quota, and eventually hit the trial-exhausted wall — even though you're already billing them.

With `isSubscriber` wired:

1. On every request the SDK calls `isSubscriber(email)` against your user DB.
2. A truthy return disables the quota gate for this request.
3. The SDK reports the conversion to With.in so the user's lead record flips to `subscriber` — next token issued carries `tier: 'subscriber'` and no quota claim at all.

The lookup should be fast (your own DB, already indexed on email). If it throws, the SDK falls back to trial behavior and logs the error.

---

## Trial tiers

`within_trial_tier` on the token claim is one of:

| Tier | Quota | Who gets it |
|------|-------|-------------|
| `subscriber` | **not enforced by With.in** | Proven vendor customer. Your own plan rules apply on your server. |
| `standard` | 20 per 14 days | Business email |
| `limited` | 10 per 7 days | Personal email (gmail, etc.) |

**Subscribers don't have a With.in-enforced quota.** The token carries no `within_calls_remaining` claim; the SDK passes those calls straight through to your handler. Any usage caps for paying customers are your business logic, on your MCP server.

---

## What the handler does (sealed pipeline)

Every `/mcp` request runs these steps in order — no opt-out:

1. **Authenticate** the Bearer token. `vendorTokenValidator` runs first; a non-null return short-circuits With.in and treats the user as subscriber (plus auto-reports conversion). Otherwise, validate against With.in's JWKS.
2. **Subscriber cross-check.** Call `isSubscriber(email)` if configured; truthy disables the quota gate for this request and reports conversion.
3. **Trial-quota enforcement.** On `tools/call`, compare `within_calls_remaining` against a per-token counter. Tokens without that claim (subscribers) are never gated. SDK-injected network tools (`find_vendor_for_task`, `within__use`) never count. If exhausted, return a vendor-branded JSON-RPC error with an upgrade URL — and live-check With.in in case the user paid seconds ago.
4. **Usage reporting.** Every `tools/call` is reported with the MCP client's `clientInfo.name`, so the dashboards show which agent (Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT…) made the call.
5. **Session management.** Maintains `mcp-session-id` → transport map. Stale sessions re-initialize cleanly.
6. **Network-tool injection.** Injects `find_vendor_for_task` + `within__use` into every session and auto-reports your tool list to With.in so other vendors' agents can find you.
7. **Transport dispatch.** Builds a Web `Request`, wraps `transport.handleRequest` in an `AsyncLocalStorage` context so network tools see the caller's auth, streams the response.

---

## What the three handlers do

| Handler | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| `prmHandler` | Serves RFC 9728 PRM at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`. Derives `resource` from the live request so byte-equality checks pass under any hostname. |
| `mcpHandler` | Runs the 7-step pipeline above for every `/mcp` request. |
| `subscribeHandler` | Extracts `?c=<code>` and 302s to `{authServerUrl}/oauth/upgrade/{vendorSlug}?c=<code>`. Exists so the upgrade link reads as `your-domain.com/subscribe?c=...` — MCP clients trust your domain, not With.in's. Codes are opaque, single-use, server-minted. |

---

## Injected tools

Every session also exposes, alongside your own tools:

- `find_vendor_for_task(intent: string)` — semantic search over the With.in vendor network. Returns 3–5 other vendors whose tools match the intent.
- `within__use(target_vendor_slug, tool_name, tool_arguments, user_confirmed)` — silently provisions a trial token for the target vendor and proxies the call. `user_confirmed=true` required as an agent guardrail.

Neither counts against your trial quota.

---

## Config reference

| Field | Required | Default | Purpose |
|------|-----|---------|------|
| `jwksUrl` | ✓ | — | With.in JWKS endpoint for token signature verification. |
| `authServerUrl` | ✓ | — | With.in base URL. Used for reporting, subscribe redirect, live subscription checks. |
| `vendorSlug` | ✓ | — | Your slug from the With.in portal. |
| `resourceUrl` | ✓ | — | Your MCP resource URL. Used in `WWW-Authenticate` on 401. |
| `zod` | ✓ | — | Your zod module (v4). |
| `createMcpServer` | ✓ | — | Factory returning a new `McpServer` per session. |
| `isSubscriber` | **strongly recommended** | — | `(email) => boolean`. Without this, paying customers get trial-walled. See above. |
| `vendorHostUrl` | recommended | — | Your public base URL — enables vendor-branded subscribe links. |
| `subscribePath` | — | `/subscribe` | Path where you mount `subscribeHandler`. |
| `vendorSignupUrl` | — | — | Fallback when the token has no `within_upgrade_url`. |
| `vendorTokenValidator` | — | — | Your own token validator. Non-null return → treat as subscriber. |
| `extractEmail` | — | — | Pull email from a vendor-native token. Default chain: `email` → `user_email` → `preferred_username` → `upn` → `sub`. |
| `scopesSupported` | — | `['tools:read', 'tools:write']` | PRM scopes. |
| `bearerMethodsSupported` | — | `['header']` | PRM bearer methods. |
| `log` | — | `() => {}` | Diagnostic log callback. |

---

## Anti-cheat (what vendors can't do)

- **Can't forge tokens.** RS256-signed, JWKS-validated.
- **Can't hide conversions.** Email extraction + conversion reporting is automatic on every auth path.
- **Can't skip the pipeline.** The integration is one handler call; there are no overrideable steps.
- **Can't mint upgrade codes.** Handoff codes are opaque, server-minted, single-use.
- **Can't hide revenue share.** Stripe Connect `invoice.paid` events flow out-of-band to With.in's webhook regardless of SDK behavior.

---

## Troubleshooting

**"No tools available" in Claude.** Deployed SDK SHA likely stale. Force a clean install (`npm ci`) and clear Render's build cache.

**`Cannot read properties of undefined (reading '_zod')`** on `tools/list`. Zod version mismatch. Pin `"zod": "^4.3.0"`.

**`Server not initialized`** on cross-vendor `within__use`. The target vendor runs an older SDK SHA without the Streamable HTTP handshake. Ask them to bump.

---

## Testing

```bash
npm install && npm test
```

A single `src/quota.test.ts` file exercises the full handler pipeline against a stubbed With.in JWKS + introspection server. Zero network, zero cost, deterministic.

Coverage: Bearer auth (missing + invalid), quota gating (`N` succeed → `N+1` trial-exhausted), network-tool exemption, live subscription flip mid-token, dashboard-initiated revoke, per-user vs per-identity counter scope, MCP Streamable HTTP session handling (unknown + stale session-id), `isSubscriber` callback short-circuit, subscriber-tier tokens never exhausting.

Last run:

```
✔ rejects request with no Bearer token
✔ rejects request with invalid token
✔ N tools/call succeed, call N+1 returns trial-exhausted payload
✔ SDK-injected network tools are excluded from the quota counter
✔ live subscription flip unblocks exhausted token
✔ dashboard-initiated revoke blocks the next tool call
✔ isSubscriber callback short-circuits the trial gate
✔ separate users have independent quota counters
✔ same identity across two tokens shares the quota counter
✔ non-initialize POST with unknown session-id returns 404 JSON-RPC error
✔ concurrent tool/calls with a stale session-id all 404 cleanly
✔ subscriber-tier tokens never exhaust
ℹ tests 12  pass 12  fail 0  duration_ms 807
```

To gate every `git push` on green tests:

```bash
cat > .git/hooks/pre-push <<'EOF'
#!/bin/sh
cd "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)" && npm test
EOF
chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-push
```
